refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-006 (Refined): The NGHE solution shall generate and maintain a real-time worksite model representing terrain elevation, static obstacles (rocks, structures, vegetation), dynamic hazards (personnel, other equipment), and designated work zones with sufficient fidelity to support autonomous path planning and operator situational awareness.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague on scope (what constitutes 'area analysis') and lacked specificity on fidelity requirements, update cadence, and consumer functions. Refinement adds measurable attributes: real-time update capability, hazard classification (static vs. dynamic), and explicit linkage to downstream planning/execution. High safety criticality demands clarity on what 'representation' means operationally.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Acceptance: (1) Worksite model generated within 5 minutes of site entry; (2) Terrain elevation accuracy ±0.5 m over 95% of mapped area; (3) Obstacles detected and classified (static/dynamic) with ≥90% recall; (4) Model available to planning and execution subsystems via agreed interface; (5) Model updates propagate to dependent functions within 2 seconds of sensor detection.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire and fuse multi-sensor data (LiDAR, camera, radar) to construct terrain and obstacle point clouds.
CR-NGHE-006.2: Classify and segment detected features into terrain, static obstacles, dynamic hazards, and work zones.
CR-NGHE-006.3: Maintain and update a persistent 3D worksite model with real-time refresh.
CR-NGHE-006.4: Expose worksite model to planning and execution functions via standardized query/subscription interface.
CR-NGHE-006.5: Validate model completeness and accuracy against acceptance criteria before release to dependent functions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized perception pipeline (sensor fusion → feature extraction → 3D reconstruction → model store) with publish-subscribe interface for downstream consumers.
DP-B: Distributed sensor processing (each sensor type processed independently, results merged at query time) with eventual consistency model.
DP-C: Hybrid: real-time fusion for safety-critical features (dynamic hazards), eventual consistency for static terrain; dual-path publication.
DP-D: Outsource worksite modeling to third-party mapping service (e.g., drone survey, cloud-based reconstruction); NGHE integrates results via API.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized perception pipeline with publish-subscribe interface. Rationale: High safety criticality and real-time update requirement (2 s propagation) demand strong consistency guarantees and deterministic latency. Centralized architecture enables unified validation, version control, and audit trail. Publish-subscribe decouples producers (sensors) from consumers (planning, execution, safety) and supports multiple simultaneous subscribers without coupling. DP-B introduces consistency risks unacceptable for safety. DP-C adds complexity without clear benefit given DP-A's feasibility. DP-D introduces external dependency and latency unsuitable for dynamic hazard detection.

potential_sources: Sensor fusion algorithm depends on calibration data (camera-LiDAR extrinsics, radar cross-section models); must be versioned and validated.
Model store schema must be stable; changes to feature representation require coordination with all subscribers.
Real-time constraint (2 s propagation) couples perception pipeline latency to downstream function timing budgets; requires end-to-end latency analysis.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are manageable via interface contracts (schema versioning, latency SLAs) and configuration management. No fundamental design contradiction detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Multi-sensor data streams are synchronized (hardware or software timestamping). Risk: Clock drift or dropped frames degrade fusion quality. Mitigation: Implement robust time-sync protocol and frame-drop detection.

Assumption: Sensor suite (LiDAR, camera, radar) provides sufficient coverage and fidelity for terrain and obstacle detection. Risk: Occlusions, weather, or sensor failures create blind spots. Mitigation: Sensor redundancy, failure detection, and graceful degradation strategy.

Assumption: Publish-subscribe middleware (e.g., ROS 2, DDS) is available and meets real-time latency requirements. Risk: Middleware overhead or network congestion violates 2 s SLA. Mitigation: Latency profiling, QoS tuning, and fallback to direct IPC if needed.

Assumption: Worksite model can be represented in a single unified format (e.g., octree, voxel grid, point cloud + semantic labels). Risk: Format may not suit all downstream consumers (e.g., path planner prefers occupancy grid, safety monitor prefers semantic labels). Mitigation: Provide multiple views/projections of the model via query interface.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the worksite model representation format and query interface contract before detailed design. Specify: (1) Coordinate system (global vs. vehicle-relative); (2) Feature schema (what attributes are stored for each detected object); (3) Query API (e.g., 'get obstacles in region', 'subscribe to dynamic hazards'); (4) Latency and consistency guarantees. This will reduce coupling risk and enable parallel development of perception and consumer subsystems.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: The Vehicle module owns the perception pipeline, sensor fusion, and worksite model generation. It acquires raw sensor data (LiDAR, camera, radar), performs feature extraction and 3D reconstruction, maintains the persistent model store, and publishes model updates via standardized interfaces. The Vehicle module is responsible for sensor calibration, data validation, and model versioning.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle publishes worksite model state (terrain, obstacles, work zones) as telemetry to downstream consumers (planning, execution, safety). Includes model version, timestamp, and feature updates.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives task/query requests from planning and execution functions (e.g., 'get obstacles in region', 'subscribe to dynamic hazards'). Tasking interface also carries sensor configuration commands (e.g., 'focus LiDAR on zone X').

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle publishes safety-critical alerts derived from worksite model (e.g., 'personnel detected in work zone', 'terrain instability detected'). Alerts are high-priority and time-stamped.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle communicates with Teleoperation and Safety Services modules via network link to share model updates and receive remote commands/queries.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Worksite modeling is a core capability but not the sole responsibility. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse ownership and blur accountability for sensor integration and model maintenance. Not recommended as primary owner.

Vehicle is the natural owner: it carries the sensors (LiDAR, camera, radar), performs real-time perception, and maintains the model for autonomous navigation and operator awareness. Sensor fusion and model generation are core Vehicle functions. High containment, minimal diffusion. Recommended.

Teleoperation is a consumer of the worksite model (for remote operator situational awareness) but not the producer. Assigning IM to Teleoperation would create a dependency inversion: remote operator would be responsible for generating the model, which is illogical. Not recommended.

Safety Services consumes worksite model to detect hazards and enforce safety policies. However, Safety Services does not own sensor acquisition or fusion. Assigning IM to Safety Services would split perception (Vehicle) from model generation (Safety Services), creating coupling and diffusion. Not recommended as primary owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the optimal owner for worksite modeling because: (1) It owns the sensor hardware (LiDAR, camera, radar) and their calibration; (2) Real-time perception and model generation are core Vehicle functions required for autonomous navigation; (3) The model is generated on-board the Vehicle and used locally before being published to remote consumers; (4) Sensor fusion and 3D reconstruction are Vehicle-specific algorithms tightly coupled to Vehicle kinematics and sensor geometry. Containment score 0.95 reflects high alignment.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle module may become overloaded if it also owns path planning, motion control, and other autonomous functions. Mitigation: Clearly separate perception (worksite modeling) from planning/control via well-defined interfaces. Use publish-subscribe to decouple.

Risk: Worksite model may need to be shared with remote Teleoperation and Safety Services modules. If Vehicle is the sole owner, it must reliably publish updates over network link. Mitigation: Implement robust network protocol with acknowledgment and retry logic.

Risk: Model validation and versioning must be consistent across Vehicle and remote consumers. Mitigation: Implement model versioning scheme and validation rules in Vehicle; publish version info with each model update.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Vehicle module has sufficient computational capacity (CPU, memory, power) to run the perception pipeline in real-time without impacting autonomous navigation. If not, consider a dedicated perception sub-module within Vehicle (e.g., 'Perception Engine') with clear interface to navigation/control. Also, specify the network protocol and QoS for publishing worksite model to remote consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services) to ensure 2 s propagation SLA.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Selected DP (centralized perception pipeline with publish-subscribe) directly addresses refined FR requirements: (1) Real-time worksite model generation ✓; (2) Terrain, obstacle, and work-zone representation ✓; (3) Availability to planning/execution functions ✓; (4) Real-time update propagation ✓. DP is feasible, well-understood, and aligns with high safety criticality. No fundamental mismatch detected.

diffusion_risks: Vehicle module may accumulate multiple responsibilities (perception, navigation, control, diagnostics). Mitigation: Establish clear sub-module boundaries within Vehicle (e.g., Perception Engine, Navigation Engine, Control Engine) with explicit interfaces.

Worksite model must be published to multiple remote consumers (Teleoperation, Safety Services) over network link. Risk: Network latency or congestion violates 2 s SLA. Mitigation: Implement QoS policies, latency monitoring, and fallback strategies.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module containment score 0.95 is high; diffusion score 0.05 is low. This indicates strong alignment between DP and IM. The four external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are all standard and well-defined, reducing integration risk. No new interfaces need to be invented.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-006 is feasible via: (1) Simulation: inject synthetic sensor data, verify model generation and update latency; (2) Hardware-in-the-loop: connect real sensors to Vehicle module, measure end-to-end latency and model accuracy; (3) Field trial: deploy Vehicle at test site, compare generated model to ground truth (e.g., surveyed terrain, known obstacles). Acceptance criteria are measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle module owns worksite modeling end-to-end (sensor acquisition → fusion → model store → publication). Downstream consumers (planning, execution, safety) receive model via Telemetry and Tasking interfaces. No ambiguity in responsibility or interface ownership.

recommended_revisions: Refine the worksite model representation format and query interface contract (coordinate system, feature schema, query API, latency/consistency SLAs) before detailed design.
Specify the network protocol and QoS for publishing worksite model to remote consumers to ensure 2 s propagation SLA.
Establish sub-module boundaries within Vehicle (Perception Engine, Navigation Engine, Control Engine) to manage complexity and reduce diffusion risk.
Define sensor calibration and validation procedures to ensure model accuracy and consistency.
Implement model versioning and audit trail for traceability and safety compliance.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-006 is not atomic; it comprises five sub-FRs (sensor acquisition, feature classification, model maintenance, interface exposure, validation). Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to design parameters and implementation modules. Proceeding to the next level will clarify the detailed architecture of the perception pipeline and reduce design risk. The FR-DP-IM gates all pass, indicating the current level is sound and ready for refinement.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire and fuse multi-sensor data (LiDAR, camera, radar) to construct terrain and obstacle point clouds.
CR-NGHE-006.2: Classify and segment detected features into terrain, static obstacles, dynamic hazards, and work zones.
CR-NGHE-006.3: Maintain and update a persistent 3D worksite model with real-time refresh.
CR-NGHE-006.4: Expose worksite model to planning and execution functions via standardized query/subscription interface.
CR-NGHE-006.5: Validate model completeness and accuracy against acceptance criteria before release to dependent functions.

assumptions: Multi-sensor data streams are synchronized (hardware or software timestamping).
Sensor suite (LiDAR, camera, radar) provides sufficient coverage and fidelity for terrain and obstacle detection.
Publish-subscribe middleware (e.g., ROS 2, DDS) is available and meets real-time latency requirements.
Worksite model can be represented in a single unified format (e.g., octree, voxel grid, point cloud + semantic labels).
Vehicle module has sufficient computational capacity to run perception pipeline in real-time without impacting autonomous navigation.
Network link is reliable and meets 2 s propagation SLA for model updates to remote consumers.
open_risks: Sensor calibration drift over time may degrade model accuracy. Mitigation: Implement online calibration and periodic validation.
Occlusions, weather, or sensor failures may create blind spots in the worksite model. Mitigation: Sensor redundancy, failure detection, and graceful degradation.
Network latency or congestion may violate 2 s propagation SLA. Mitigation: QoS tuning, latency monitoring, and fallback strategies.
Worksite model format may not suit all downstream consumers. Mitigation: Provide multiple views/projections via query interface.
Vehicle module may become overloaded with multiple responsibilities. Mitigation: Establish clear sub-module boundaries and interfaces.
evidence_needed: Sensor specifications and calibration procedures (LiDAR, camera, radar).
Sensor fusion algorithm design and validation results (accuracy, latency).
Worksite model representation format and query interface specification.
Network protocol and QoS specification for model publication.
Computational capacity analysis for Vehicle module (CPU, memory, power).
End-to-end latency analysis (sensor acquisition → model generation → publication → consumer processing).
Field trial results comparing generated model to ground truth.
